title: Data Representativeness Testing
concept:
For an ML model to generalise to real-world conditions, its training and test data must be **representative** of the operational population it will serve. Unrepresentative data is one of the most common causes of AI system failure after deployment.

**Step 1: Define the target population**
- Understand the operational context: What real-world inputs will the system encounter?
- Consult domain experts to identify important subgroups and edge cases.
- Apply stratified sampling: ensure each subgroup of the population is represented in proportion to its real-world frequency.
- Document the population definition for audit purposes.

**Step 2: Analyse data characteristics**
- Perform EDA on both training data and a reference dataset (e.g., operational data samples).
- Visualise and compare distributions of key features between training data and operational data.
- Examine correlations between features.
- Identify anomalies and unexpected patterns.

**Step 3: Apply statistical tests**
- **Chi-squared test**: Tests whether categorical variable distributions differ significantly between training and reference datasets.
- **Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) test**: Tests whether two continuous variable distributions are drawn from the same distribution.
- A statistically significant difference indicates the training data does not represent the operational population.

**Post-deployment monitoring**: After deployment, continuously monitor the distribution of incoming operational data. If distributions shift (data drift), representativeness is compromised and retraining may be needed.
keyPoints: Data representativeness testing verifies that training/test data distributions match the target operational population — using statistical tests (Chi-squared for categorical, K-S for continuous data).
Stratified sampling ensures subgroups are represented proportionally; domain experts help identify which subgroups are operationally important.
Representativeness must be monitored continuously post-deployment because real-world data distributions change over time (data drift).

quiz.question: A credit-scoring model was trained on data collected between 2015 and 2020. In 2024, the team wants to verify the training data still represents the current applicant population. Which statistical test should they apply to numerical features like annual income?
options: Chi-squared test — it is the standard test for all feature types in ML datasets.
Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) test — it tests whether two continuous distributions (2015-2020 training data vs 2024 operational data) are significantly different.
Pearson correlation test — it checks whether annual income correlates with loan default rates.
F1-score comparison — it measures whether model accuracy has changed on the 2024 data.
quiz.explanation: The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test is used to compare two continuous distributions — exactly the case here (historical training distribution vs current operational distribution of annual income). The Chi-squared test is for categorical variables. Pearson correlation tests feature-outcome relationships, not distribution similarity. F1 comparison tests model performance, not data representativeness.
